3A_Bibliographic.citation: Hamilton, J. L., Hutchinson, E., Evankovich, M. R., Ladouceur, C. D., & Silk, J. S. (2024). Daily and average associations of physical activity, social media use, and sleep among adolescent girls during the COVID-19 pandemic. Sleep, 48(1), zsae234.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: Not ABCD data - uses separate longitudinal study of adolescent girls
3B_Sample_Data.timepoints_used: N/A - daily diary data collected over 2 weeks at 2 time points
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: Participants included 143 adolescent girls (ages 11-17) who completed daily diary assessments over two 2-week periods during the COVID-19 pandemic.
3B_Sample_Data.notes: THIS PAPER DOES NOT USE ABCD DATA. It is a separate study of adolescent girls using daily diary methods. Should be flagged for exclusion from ABCD-specific review.

3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Participants reported the number of minutes they spent on social media each day during the daily diary period.

3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: Daily diary self-report of sleep duration and quality
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Sleep duration was calculated from self-reported bedtime and wake time. Sleep quality was rated daily on a scale.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Multilevel models with daily observations nested within persons
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, day of week, time point, person-level averages
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multilevel models were estimated with daily observations (Level 1) nested within persons (Level 2). Both within-person (daily fluctuations) and between-person (average levels) associations were examined.

3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: On days when girls spent more time on social media than their own average, they slept less that night (B = -0.004, 95% CI: -0.007, -0.001)

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: Greater daily social media use was significantly associated with shorter sleep duration
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: Effect sizes were small, suggesting that while statistically significant, the practical significance of daily social media use on sleep may be limited
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: NOT ABCD DATA - flagged for exclusion

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Findings support recommendations to limit social media use before bedtime to support healthy sleep in adolescent girls.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: Social media use and physical activity represent modifiable behaviors that could be targeted to improve adolescent sleep
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames daily social media use as a modifiable risk factor for sleep in adolescent girls. Acknowledges small effect sizes and the need for nuanced understanding of within-person versus between-person processes. Notes that the COVID-19 context may limit generalizability.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: NOT ABCD DATA. Paper was likely included in candidate list by mistake. Uses a separate sample of 143 adolescent girls.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.notes: THIS PAPER DOES NOT USE ABCD DATA. Should be flagged for exclusion.
